name: Nasir al-Mulk Mosque
description: Known as the 'Pink Mosque' for its extensive rose-colored tiles, Nasir al-Mulk is famed for its breathtaking stained-glass windows that cast a kaleidoscope of colors across the prayer hall at sunrise. Built between 1876 and 1888 during the Qajar era, it is one of the most photographed mosques in the world.

name: Vakil Bazaar
description: A sprawling covered market dating from the 18th-century Zand dynasty, Vakil Bazaar is the commercial heart of Shiraz with wide brick-vaulted arcades and elegant courtyards. The complex includes the Vakil Bath, Vakil Mosque, and a caravanserai, and is renowned for its spice merchants, carpet dealers, and traditional crafts.

name: Si-o-se-pol Bridge
description: The 'Bridge of 33 Arches' is a 298-meter-long double-story bridge built in 1602 under Shah Abbas I, serving as both a bridge and a dam across the Zayanderud River. At night, illuminated arches reflecting in the water create one of Isfahan's most iconic vistas, and the lower walkways serve as a beloved gathering place for locals.
visiting_hours: Open 24 hours (best at sunset and after dark)

name: Shah Cheragh
description: A funerary monument and mosque housing the tomb of brothers Ahmad and Muhammad, sons of the seventh Shia Imam Musa al-Kadhim. The interior is a dazzling mirror-work masterpiece with millions of tiny mirror fragments covering every surface, creating an otherworldly glittering effect that ranks among Iran's most extraordinary sacred spaces.
entrance_fee: Free (guided tours for non-Muslims, chador required for women)

name: Tomb of Hafez
description: The memorial to the beloved 14th-century Persian poet Hafez, set within a serene garden in the heart of Shiraz. The open-sided pavilion with its elegant copper dome shelters Hafez's alabaster tomb, and the gardens are a gathering place where Iranians come to read his poetry, seek divination (fal-e Hafez), and pay homage to their literary heritage.

name: Tomb of Saadi
description: The mausoleum of the celebrated 13th-century Persian poet Saadi Shirazi, whose works 'Bustan' and 'Golestan' are cornerstones of Persian literature. The tomb sits in a garden with a turquoise-tiled portico and a subterranean pool fed by a qanat, and his verse 'Children of Adam' adorns the entrance hall of the United Nations.

name: Milad Tower
description: Standing 435 meters tall, Milad Tower is the sixth-tallest freestanding tower in the world and Tehran's most recognizable modern landmark. The tower features a revolving restaurant, observation deck with panoramic views of the Alborz Mountains, an art gallery, and a convention center at its base.

name: Azadi Tower
description: Originally built in 1971 as the 'Shahyad Tower' to commemorate 2,500 years of the Persian Empire, this 45-meter inverted Y-shaped monument clad in white Isfakhan marble is Tehran's gateway icon. Blending Sassanid and Islamic architectural elements, the tower houses a museum and offers views from its observation deck over Azadi Square.

name: Tabiat Bridge
description: A striking 270-meter pedestrian overpass designed by architect Leila Araghian, connecting two parks across a Tehran highway. Completed in 2014, it won the Aga Khan Award for Architecture and has become Tehran's most popular public space, with three levels of walkways, seating areas, restaurants, and green spaces.

name: Mount Damavand
description: At 5,671 meters, Damavand is the highest peak in Iran and the Middle East, and the highest volcano in Asia. This potentially active stratovolcano dominates the central Alborz range and holds deep significance in Persian mythology as the place where the dragon Azi Dahaka was imprisoned. It is a popular mountaineering destination with a summit season from June to September.
entrance_fee: Climbing permit ~3,000,000 IRR; guide fees extra
visiting_hours: Climbing season June-September; base camp accessible year-round

name: Badab-e Surt
description: A series of terraced travertine hot springs formed over thousands of years by two mineral-rich springs with different compositions. The iron oxide and carbonate deposits create striking orange, red, and yellow terraces reminiscent of Turkey's Pamukkale but with a unique reddish hue, set at 1,840 meters elevation in the Alborz foothills.
visiting_hours: Sunrise to sunset (best light at sunrise/sunset)

name: Rainbow Mountains (Aladaglar)
description: Spectacularly layered sedimentary mountains displaying vivid bands of red, orange, yellow, green, and purple minerals, often called Iran's 'Rainbow Mountains.' Located in northwest Iran near Tabriz, these geological formations date back millions of years and have become increasingly popular with photographers and hikers seeking surreal natural landscapes.
visiting_hours: Daylight hours only; accessible spring through autumn

name: Qeshm Island UNESCO Global Geopark
description: Iran's largest island hosts a UNESCO-recognized geopark featuring extraordinary geological formations including the Chahkooh Canyon, Namakdan Salt Cave (the longest salt cave in the world), and mangrove forests (Hara). The geopark covers the western half of Qeshm and showcases 250 million years of Earth's geological history.

name: Stars Valley
description: A dramatic erosion-carved canyon on Qeshm Island with towering pillars and columns sculpted by wind and rain over millions of years. Local folklore attributes the formations to a fallen star, and the maze-like corridors between rock spires create an eerie, otherworldly atmosphere, especially at sunset when the rocks glow golden.

name: Dasht-e Kavir
description: Iran's largest desert, spanning over 77,000 square kilometers of salt flats, sand dunes, and mud plains across the central Iranian plateau. Known as the Great Salt Desert, its mesmerizing salt polygons, star dunes, and vast emptiness offer a raw, pristine desert experience best accessed via the oasis towns of Jandaq or Mesr.
visiting_hours: Best visited October-April; avoid summer heat

name: Alisadr Cave
description: The world's largest water cave, Alisadr features an underground lake system navigated by pedal boats through illuminated chambers of stalactites and stalagmites. Formed in Jurassic-era limestone over 130 million years ago, the cave extends at least 2.5 kilometers and prehistoric paintings on its walls suggest human habitation 12,000 years ago.

name: Maranjab Desert and Caravanserai
description: A pristine desert region near Kashan featuring a beautifully restored Safavid-era caravanserai, a salt lake, and towering sand dunes. Maranjab is one of Iran's most accessible desert destinations, popular for stargazing, sandboarding, and overnight camping where the silence and clarity of the night sky are extraordinary.

name: Anzali Lagoon
description: A Ramsar-listed freshwater lagoon on the Caspian coast covering approximately 193 square kilometers, famous for its lotus lily fields that bloom spectacularly from June to September. The wetland is a critical habitat for migratory birds including pelicans, herons, and cormorants, and local fishermen navigate the waterways in traditional wooden boats.
